title: Operational Performance and Traffic Analysis of Turkey’s National Aviation Network
text:
This project aims to analyze the operational performance and passenger traffic trends across all civil airports in Turkey from 2018 to 2025. Instead of focusing only on a single city, this study will examine the entire national aviation network. The scope includes identifying growth patterns in different regions, analyzing the seasonality of tourism-based airports (such as Antalya and Dalaman), and measuring the efficiency of regional airports compared to major hubs like Istanbul (IST) and Ankara (ESB).
Objectives:

To benchmark airport efficiency using “Passenger/Flight” ratios.
To analyze the seasonal sensitivity of regional vs. hub airports.
To visualize the geographical distribution of air traffic growth.

title: Operational Performance and Traffic Analysis of Turkey’s National Aviation Network
text:
2.1 Data Source
The dataset is retrieved from the official statistics of the General Directorate of State Airports Authority (DHMİ).

Reference: DHMİ Statistics dhmi.gov.tr/Pages/Statistics.aspx

title: Operational Performance and Traffic Analysis of Turkey’s National Aviation Network
text:
2.2 General Information About Data
The dataset includes monthly and yearly reports for more than 50 airports. Key variables to be analyzed are:

Passenger Numbers: Total counts for domestic and international flights.
Flight Movements: The number of commercial and non-commercial aircraft taking off and landing.
Cargo and Freight: The volume of air cargo handled, which reflects the economic activity of the region. The data allows for a longitudinal study, covering the period before, during, and after the global pandemic.

title: Operational Performance and Traffic Analysis of Turkey’s National Aviation Network
text:
2.3 Reason of Choice
Analyzing the full national dataset is essential for a complete understanding of Turkey's logistics and transportation infrastructure. This choice allows for benchmarking (comparing different airports) to see which ones are operating at high efficiency and which ones have underutilized capacity. From an Industrial Engineering perspective, this wide-scale data enables the use of clustering techniques to group airports by their traffic behavior and forecasting models to predict future demand across different regions of Turkey.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Task 3

(a)
video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fi8-phj1X1A&t
Dr. Cem Vardar explains the relationship between data science and industrial engineering. He defines industrial engineering as a job that solves complex problems with science and mathematics. His approach to solve complex problems and optimizing the efficiency of a system is “don’t touch if it works” principle. This principle solves the issues step by step, improving each time. According to Dr. Cem Vardar, this approach is way more succesful than creating a new complex tool for each problem. Dr. Cem categorizes the demand for people which deals with data:

General Data Scientist/Analyst
Predictive Modelling
Machine Learning
OR Data Scientist

Dr. Cem Vardar also recommends that social and analytical skills are crucial for an engineer. In his speech, it is stated that these soft skills are as important as technical skills and it is always a plus for an engineer to utilize various skillsets.


(b)
Q1) In his presentation, Dr. Cem Vardar mentions evolutionary design and the “if it works, don’t touch it” principle when dealing with complex systems. If an engineer wants to improve a system that has problems but is still running (like a logistics network getting customer complaints), how should they approach it according to Dr. Cem Vardar? Also, what are the main risks of throwing everything and starting from scratch?
Answer: According to Dr. Cem Vardar, an engineer should avoid throwing away a working system just to try and build a “perfect” one from zero. Even if the system has issues, it is still functioning. Instead, problem should be broken into small manageable parts and be solved spep by step using evolutionary approach. Main points should be identified and small modest improvements should be made in each step.
Q2) When we consider Dr. Cem Vardar’s analytical thinking methods, which is the best approacg for an industrial engineer with small amount of data?

Complex mathematical models and machine learning algorithms should be integrated to the system without thinking about small data.
Purchasing only high budget automation systems instead of using statistical models to fill gaps in databases.
Using logic and simple tools (like averages) that fit the problem better.
Doing nothing for years because you think you don’t have enough data to solve the problem.
Mixing random data from different departments just to make your data look bigger.

Anser: C


(c)
Package “dslabs” is installed.

#install.packages("dslabs")
library(dslabs)

Data “mtcars” is installed and checked with print function. mpg column of the dataset is now mpg object which is a vector of its own. compute stats function is defined and it finds the demanded values of the mpg.

mpg <- mtcars$mpg

compute_stats <- function(num_vector){
  
  statslist <- c(
    mean = mean(num_vector),
    median = median(num_vector),
    variance = var(num_vector),
    iqr = IQR(num_vector),
    minimum = min(num_vector),
    maximum = max(num_vector)
    )
  return(statslist)
}

#instead of c(), we can use list() but it is harder for apply function to make it a matrix.

compute_stats(mpg)

    mean   median variance      iqr  minimum  maximum 
20.09062 19.20000 36.32410  7.37500 10.40000 33.90000 


using for loop with compute_stats to find the values for all of the columns in mtcars dataset.

for (i in names(mtcars)){
  print(i)
  cat("\n")
  print(compute_stats(mtcars[[i]]))
  cat("\n")
}

my_first and my_birth_year is defined and also k value is found. Dataset is trimmed according to k being odd or even.

my_first <- "Akif Bora"
my_birth_year <- 2001

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8

if (k%%2 ==0) {
  trimmed_data <- head(polls_us_election_2016, k)
} else{
  trimmed_data <- tail(polls_us_election_2016, k)
}

Replacing NA values in the trimmed_data with the demanded element in the task.

#number of na values in the entire dataset
sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))

[1] 11604

#replacing the na values

for (i in names(trimmed_data)) {
  column <- trimmed_data[[i]]

  if (is.numeric(column)) {
    column[is.na(column)] <- my_birth_year + k
  } 
  else if (is.character(column) || is.factor(column)) {
    column <- as.character(column)

    column[is.na(column)] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
  else if (is.factor(column) || is.factor(column)) {
    column <- as.character(column)
  }
  trimmed_data[[i]] <- column
}
print(trimmed_data)
